blocks.content: A focused refresher for the **BaseKit PHP Backend Developer — Round 2** interview. Each section opens with *why it matters*, then gives the concept in plain English and a PHP example you can speak to. Read it once end-to-end, then drill the topics that came up in round one.

1. PHP 8 vs PHP 7
2. Dependency Injection
3. SOLID Principles
9. OOP Concepts

4. MySQL Performance
5. REST API Design
10. Security Essentials

6. Docker & Kubernetes
7. Linux / Mac CLI
8. Git Workflow
11. Best Practices
**How to use it:** skim the headings, read every *Why it matters* box, and rehearse saying each idea out loud. The phrases on the final page are the ones to drop into answers.
PHP 8 (Nov 2020) was a major release — big performance gains and real language features, built on by 8.1 and 8.2. Coming from WordPress / PHP 7, these are the things you need to know cold.
Pass arguments *by name*, in any order, skipping optional ones.
Declare that a value can be one of several types.
Collapses nested null checks — if any link is null, the chain returns null.
Like `switch`, but it **returns a value**, uses strict `===` comparison, and has no fall-through.
A `match` with no arm and no `default` throws `UnhandledMatchError` — safer than `switch` silently doing nothing.
Declare and assign properties in one line — great for DTOs and value objects.
Set once at initialisation, immutable thereafter.
Native enumerations — no more class constants or magic strings.
Lightweight concurrency — pause and resume execution without a full event loop. Know *what* they are and *why* they exist; you won't be asked to write one.
Cleaner way to pass a function as a callable:

$fn = strlen(...);
array_map(strlen(...), $strings);
Union = *this OR that*; intersection = *this AND that* (must satisfy all). A `readonly` class makes **every** property readonly by default.

BaseKit's job ads call out DI as a core practice, and they use it heavily on the React frontend too. Being able to articulate *why* DI is good — testability, loose coupling, single responsibility — matters as much as the how.

**Plain English:** instead of a class creating its own dependencies, you *pass them in from outside*. The class declares what it needs; something else provides it.

Like a restaurant kitchen — the chef doesn't go to the farm, ingredients are delivered. The chef just cooks.
Tightly coupled, hard to test.
Loosely coupled, testable.
Most common — required upfront.
Optional — set after construction.
The dependency supplies an injector method. Rare in practice.
Real apps wire everything together with a container — Laravel's service container, Symfony's DI component, or PHP-DI.
**Say this:** *"DI gives me loose coupling and testability — I program to interfaces, not implementations."*
BaseKit mention SOLID explicitly. Know each letter and a one-line PHP example for it — the goal of all five is small, focused classes that are easy to change.
A class should have one, and only one, reason to change.
Open for extension, closed for modification — add behaviour without editing existing code.

Subtypes must be usable anywhere their base type is, without breaking things. The classic trap: `Square extends Rectangle` forces both dimensions equal and breaks the rectangle's contract. Prefer a shared interface when behaviour differs.
Don't force classes to implement methods they don't use. Many small interfaces beat one fat one.
High-level modules shouldn't depend on low-level ones — both depend on abstractions.
This came up in round one — expect more in round two. Lead with EXPLAIN, then talk indexes, then N+1.
Run it before optimising any query.

- **type** — `const` → `ref` → `range` → `index` → `ALL` (worst). `ALL` = full table scan.
- **key** — index used (NULL = none).
- **rows** — estimated rows examined.
- **Extra** — beware `Using filesort` / `Using temporary`.

blocks.text: First thing I'd do is run EXPLAIN and check whether we're doing a full table scan.

blocks.content: A separate structure that finds rows without scanning the whole table — like a book's index. **Leftmost-prefix rule** governs composite indexes.

CREATE INDEX idx_user_status ON orders(user_id, status);
-- helps:  WHERE user_id = 1 AND status = 'active'
-- helps:  WHERE user_id = 1
-- NO help: WHERE status = 'active'   (no leftmost prefix)
**When indexes hurt:** every index slows writes (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE). Don't index blindly.
One of the most common performance killers, especially in ORMs.
In raw SQL, JOIN instead of looping:

SET GLOBAL slow_query_log = 'ON';
SET GLOBAL long_query_time = 1;   -- log queries over 1 second

# Summarise: sort by time, top 10
mysqldumpslow -s t -t 10 /var/log/mysql/mysql-slow.log
Fetches columns you don't need. Select only what you use.
`LIKE '%x%'` can't use an index. Use a FULLTEXT index for search.
**Missing WHERE on large tables** — always filter early. **Transactions** — wrap related writes so they commit together and stay consistent:

- Resources are **nouns**, not verbs
- Use HTTP **methods** to express intent
- **Stateless** — each request carries everything it needs

GET    /users      list
GET    /users/42   fetch one
POST   /users      create
PUT    /users/42   replace
PATCH  /users/42   partial update
DELETE /users/42   delete

200 OK          success
201 Created     POST created
204 No Content  DELETE ok
400 Bad Request invalid data
401 Unauthorized not auth'd
403 Forbidden   not allowed
404 Not Found
422 Unprocessable validation
429 Too Many    rate limited
500 Server Error
Always version so you can ship breaking changes without breaking clients: `/api/v1/users`, `/api/v2/users`.
BaseKit use Docker and Kubernetes. You don't need to be a DevOps expert — know the basics and the vocabulary.

- **Image** — a blueprint (like a class)
- **Container** — a running instance (like an object)
- **Dockerfile** — build instructions
- **docker-compose** — run many containers together

docker ps              # running
docker ps -a           # all
docker logs -f myapp   # tail logs
docker exec -it myapp bash
docker build -t app:latest .
docker run -d -p 8080:80 nginx

FROM php:8.2-fpm
RUN docker-php-ext-install pdo pdo_mysql
WORKDIR /var/www
COPY . .
COPY --from=composer:latest /usr/bin/composer /usr/bin/composer
RUN composer install --no-dev --optimize-autoloader
EXPOSE 9000
CMD ["php-fpm"]

services:
  app:
    build: .
    ports: ["8000:8000"]
    depends_on: [db]
    environment:
      DB_HOST: db
  db:
    image: mysql:8.0
    environment:
      MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD: secret
      MYSQL_DATABASE: myapp
    volumes:
      - db_data:/var/lib/mysql
volumes:
  db_data:

docker-compose up -d      # start all
docker-compose down       # stop all
docker-compose logs -f    # tail all

Orchestrates containers at scale. Know the four nouns:

- **Pod** — smallest unit, one or more containers
- **Deployment** — rolling updates and replicas
- **Service** — exposes pods to network traffic
- **Ingress** — routes external traffic to services
The commands that come up day-to-day as a backend dev. Mac and Linux both use bash/zsh.

pwd
ls -la
cd /var/www/html
tail -f logfile.log
grep -r "findme" ./
find . -name "*.php"

ps aux | grep php
kill 1234
kill -9 1234     # force
top
htop

chmod 755 script.sh
chmod +x script.sh
chown www-data:www-data /var/www
sudo command

curl -I https://example.com
wget https://host/file.zip
netstat -tulpn
lsof -i :8080

ssh user@host
ssh -i ~/.ssh/key user@host
scp file.txt user@host:/path

Ctrl+C   kill process
Ctrl+R   search history
!!       repeat last command
!$       last arg of prev cmd

php -v                  # version
php -m                  # modules
php -r "echo phpversion();"
php artisan migrate     # Laravel
composer install
composer dump-autoload

git status
git diff
git add .
git commit -m "message"
git push origin feature/x
git pull origin main
git log --oneline --graph

git checkout -b feature/new
git checkout main
git merge feature/new
git rebase main
git branch -d feature/new

git stash / git stash pop
git reset HEAD~1        # keep changes
git reset --hard HEAD~1 # DISCARD
git revert abc1234      # safe on shared

feat: add auth endpoint
fix: null pointer in processor
refactor: extract payment service
chore: bump composer deps
Hide internal state; expose only what's needed.
Expose *what* something does, not *how*.
Child classes extend parent behaviour.
Same call, different behaviour per class.
BaseKit explicitly prefer this. Instead of inheriting behaviour, inject it — flatter hierarchies, easier testing.
**Say this:** *"I prefer composing behaviour through injected dependencies over deep inheritance hierarchies."*
Never interpolate user input into SQL. Use prepared statements.

// SAFE echo "

Hello " . htmlspecialchars($_GET['name'], ENT_QUOTES, 'UTF-8') . "

";
Use tokens to prove a form came from your own site. Laravel does this automatically with `@csrf`.
Never plain text, MD5 or SHA1.

$hash = password_hash($pw, PASSWORD_BCRYPT);
if (password_verify($pw, $hash)) { /* ok */ }
Never commit secrets. Keep them in `.env`, and put `.env` in `.gitignore`.

- Readable in 6 months?
- Is it tested?
- Edge cases handled?
- Is there a simpler way?

- **DRY** — extract repeated logic
- **YAGNI** — don't build for needs that don't exist
- **KISS** — simplest thing that works; complexity must be earned
*If something is hard to test, it's usually a sign the design needs revisiting.*

The first half is the breadth refresher. This half goes deeper into the things a *technical* round tends to probe — language internals, data integrity, caching, async, and how a PHP app actually runs and scales.

12. Type System & Gotchas
13. Arrays & Higher-Order Fns
14. Traits / Abstract / Interface
15. Generators & Closures
16. Errors & Exceptions

17. Design Patterns
18. PSR & Composer
19. ACID & Isolation
20. Caching Strategies
21. Queues & Background Jobs

22. Auth & HTTP
23. Request Lifecycle / FPM
24. Testing Deeper
25. Big-O & Coding
26. Scaling & Multi-Tenancy
"What does this print?" is a classic technical-round opener. Type juggling and loose comparison catch out anyone who hasn't looked closely at PHP's rules — and PHP 8 quietly changed several of them.
`==` coerces types before comparing; `===` checks value **and** type. Default to `===` unless you *want* coercion.

var_dump(0 == "a");     // PHP 7: true (!)   PHP 8: false
var_dump("1" == 1);      // true  — numeric string
var_dump("abc" == 0);    // PHP 7: true        PHP 8: false
var_dump(null == false); // true
var_dump([] == false);   // true
var_dump(0 === "0");     // false — different types
**The PHP 8 fix:** comparing a number to a non-numeric string now casts the *number* to string, so `0 == "abc"` is finally `false`. Knowing this one signals you've actually used PHP 8.

$name = $data['name'] ?? 'guest';   // null coalescing — no warning if unset
$config['x'] ??= 'default';          // assign only if null/unset
$cmp = $a <=> $b;                    // spaceship: -1, 0, or 1
declare(strict_types=1);             // top of file — enforce type declarations
**Say this:** *"I put `declare(strict_types=1)` at the top of every file and use `===` by default — type juggling should be a deliberate choice, not an accident."*
PHP arrays are ordered maps — they double as lists, dictionaries, stacks and queues. Knowing the functional trio cold reads as fluency.

$nums = [1, 2, 3, 4];

$doubled = array_map(fn($n) => $n * 2, $nums);            // [2, 4, 6, 8]
$evens   = array_filter($nums, fn($n) => $n % 2 === 0);   // [1 => 2, 3 => 4]
$sum     = array_reduce($nums, fn($c, $n) => $c + $n, 0); // 10
`array_filter` **preserves keys**, leaving gaps. Reindex with `array_values()` before sending as a JSON array.

- `array_column()` — pluck a field
- `array_combine()` — keys + values
- `usort()` — custom sort with `<=>`
- `[...$a, ...$b]` — spread/merge
**`array_map` vs `foreach`:** `array_map` is declarative and returns a new array (no mutation). Reach for a `foreach` when you need the index, early exit, or side effects.
The trio shines on rows from the database. Say you've fetched a list of orders:
There's no `array_group` — you build it with a `foreach`. Worth knowing by heart:
**Sorting by a field** uses the spaceship operator inside `usort`:
usort($orders, fn($a, $b) => $b['total'] <=> $a['total']);  // highest total first
"When would you use a trait over an abstract class?" tests whether you understand composition, contracts and PHP's single-inheritance model.
A **contract** — no implementation. A class can implement many. *"Can do X."*
Partial implementation + shared state. **Single** inheritance, can't be instantiated. *"Is a kind of X."*
Horizontal **code reuse** — copy-pasted in at compile time. Use for shared behaviour across unrelated classes.
`yield` produces values one at a time without building the whole array in memory. The interview angle: *processing a huge file or result set without running out of memory*.
**Key distinction:** arrow functions (`fn`) capture outer variables automatically *by value*; classic `function () use (...)` closures capture explicitly, and `&` makes the capture by reference.
Everything throwable implements `Throwable`, which splits into **`Error`** (engine-level: `TypeError`, `DivisionByZeroError`) and **`Exception`** (application-level, the ones you throw and catch).

class InsufficientFundsException
    extends DomainException {}
Extend `DomainException`, `RuntimeException`, etc. so callers can catch by type.

- Swallow exceptions with an empty `catch`
- Catch `\Exception` everywhere
- Use exceptions for normal control flow
- Lose the original via `previous`
BaseKit value clean architecture. You don't need to recite the Gang of Four — but naming the right pattern for a problem, and knowing one anti-pattern, lands well.

rows: Hide persistence behind an interface — swap MySQL for a fake in tests.
Centralise complex object creation; return an interface, not a concrete.
Swap interchangeable behaviours (e.g. payment gateways) without if-ladders.
Let many listeners react to an event (e.g. 'order placed') without coupling.
Wrap a third-party API in your own interface so vendor changes stay contained.
Add behaviour (logging, caching) by wrapping, not subclassing.

blocks.content: Hidden global state, hard to test, hides dependencies. In modern PHP the **DI container** manages a single shared instance for you — without the downsides. Mention this trade-off if asked.
Callers depend on an *interface*, not on MySQL. Swap a fake in tests instantly.
The order doesn't know who's listening; listeners subscribe to the event.
PSRs are the shared conventions that let PHP packages interoperate. Know what the common ones cover.

- **PSR-4** — autoloading (namespace → path)
- **PSR-12** — coding style
- **PSR-3** — logger interface
- **PSR-7 / 15** — HTTP messages & middleware
- **PSR-11** — container interface

`^7.2` → any `7.x` (≥ 7.2)
`~7.2` → `7.2.x` only

`MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH` — major = breaking, minor = features, patch = fixes.

composer require monolog/monolog            # add a dependency
composer install --no-dev --optimize-autoloader   # production install
composer dump-autoload                       # rebuild the autoloader
Money, bookings, anything that must not half-happen — this is where backend rigour shows. Expect questions on transactions, locking and race conditions.

- **Atomic** — all or nothing
- **Consistent** — valid state → valid state
- **Isolated** — concurrent txns don't interfere
- **Durable** — committed data survives a crash
Use `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE` to lock rows you're about to change and avoid lost updates. *InnoDB's gap locking largely prevents phantoms even at REPEATABLE READ.*
This is the classic *"two requests at the same time"* question. If you can describe the bug **and** the fix, you sound like someone who's run production.
Imagine the last ticket to a gig. Two requests check stock at the same instant:

**Even simpler:** push the check into the write itself — `UPDATE events SET stock = stock - 1 WHERE id = 7 AND stock > 0` — then check the affected-row count. The database does the locking for you, and stock can never go negative.
*"There are only two hard things in computer science: cache invalidation and naming things."* Know the layers and the cache-aside pattern.

- **OPcache** — compiled PHP bytecode (turn on in prod)
- **App cache** — Redis / Memcached for data
- **HTTP cache** — `Cache-Control`, ETags, CDN
- **Query cache** — materialised/computed results

**Invalidation:** set a sensible **TTL**, and bust the key on write (`$redis->del("user:$id")` after an update). **Write-through** updates cache and DB together; **cache-aside** (above) is the common default. Always have a plan for a cold cache — a thundering herd of misses can take the DB down.
Anything slow — sending email, resizing images, calling a third-party API — shouldn't block the HTTP response. Knowing why and how to defer work is a core backend signal.
A **producer** pushes a job onto a **broker** (Redis, RabbitMQ, SQS); a separate **worker** process pulls and runs it asynchronously.

- **Idempotent** jobs — safe to run twice
- **Retries** with backoff
- **Dead-letter queue** for poison jobs
- Pass IDs, not whole objects
Faster responses, resilience to spikes (the queue absorbs load), and failures that retry instead of losing data. Workers scale independently of web servers.
Server stores the session; client holds a cookie. Easy to revoke; needs shared session storage (Redis) when scaled.
Signed token holds the claims: `header.payload.signature`. Nothing stored server-side — but hard to revoke before expiry. Keep them short-lived + use refresh tokens.
Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9.eyJzdWIiOiI0MiJ9.<signature>

- **OAuth2** — delegated access; *authentication* (who you are) vs *authorization* (what you can do).
- **CORS** — the browser's pre-flight `OPTIONS` check; a server header, not a security boundary.
- **Idempotency** — `GET`, `PUT`, `DELETE` are idempotent; `POST` is not. Matters for safe retries.
- **Always HTTPS**, never log tokens, hash anything secret at rest.
A favourite senior question: *"What actually happens when a request hits a PHP app?"* The shared-nothing model explains a lot of PHP's behaviour.

**nginx** receives the request → forwards to a **PHP-FPM** pool over FastCGI → an FPM **worker** bootstraps the app, handles the request, sends the response → the worker is **reset** and reused for the next request.
Every request starts with a **clean slate** — no variables persist between requests (unlike Node). So global in-memory state is useless across requests.

- Sessions/cache live in **Redis or the DB**, not memory
- **OPcache** keeps compiled bytecode hot
- Statelessness makes **horizontal scaling** trivial
**Say this:** *"PHP is shared-nothing — each request is isolated, which is why state goes in Redis or the database and why we can scale just by adding more FPM workers behind the load balancer."*
Many **unit** tests (fast, isolated), fewer **integration** tests (real DB), fewest **end-to-end** (slow, brittle). Optimise the bulk for speed.
**Arrange, Act, Assert** per test. **TDD** = red → green → refactor: write a failing test, make it pass, then clean up.

**Stub** returns canned data; **mock** also asserts it was called; **fake** is a working lightweight implementation (e.g. in-memory repo); **spy** records calls. Data providers run one test over many cases:
Coverage is a *guide*, not a goal — 100% coverage of trivial getters proves little; one good test of the money path proves a lot.

Big-O is just a way of answering one question: *as the input gets bigger, how fast does the work grow?* It's not about seconds — it's about shape. Interviewers use it to check you can reason about cost before you write code.

**The mental model.** `n` is the size of the input (number of items). Big-O describes the *number of steps* relative to `n`. We ignore constants and only keep the dominant term — `O(2n + 5)` is just **O(n)**, because when `n` is huge the rest stops mattering.
This table is the whole point. It shows roughly how many steps each class takes as the input grows. Watch what O(n²) does.

**O(1)** doesn't care how big the input is. **O(log n)** barely grows — a million items takes ~20 steps. **O(n²)** explodes — a million items is a *trillion* steps, i.e. it will never finish. That's the difference.
Same idea, one function per class. The thing to notice is the **loop shape**.
No loop — jump straight to the answer. Same cost for 10 or 10 million.

$first  = $users[0];        // index
$exists = isset($map[$key]); // hash
One loop over the input — touch each item once.

blocks.text: O(log n) · logarithmic — halve the problem each step

blocks.content: Binary search on a **sorted** array. Each step throws away half of what's left, so a million items takes only ~20 steps.

blocks.text: O(n log n) · the cost of a good sort

blocks.content:
sort($nums);                                   // ~O(n log n)
usort($users, fn($a, $b) => $a->age <=> $b->age);
**O(n²) · quadratic** — a loop *inside* a loop over the same data. This is the one to watch for: it looks innocent and dies on real data.
Compares every item against every other — 1,000 items = 1,000,000 comparisons.
Remember what you've seen in a hash map — 1,000 items = 1,000 steps.

blocks.text: HOW TO SPOT IT AT A GLANCE

blocks.content:
- **No loop**, direct access → **O(1)**
- **Halving** the search space each step → **O(log n)**
- **One loop** over the input → **O(n)**
- A **sort**, or divide-and-conquer → **O(n log n)**
- A **loop inside a loop** over the same data → **O(n²)** ⚠

Trading nested loops for a hash map — **O(n²) → O(n)** — is the single most common optimisation they look for. The classic *two-sum* shows it: instead of checking every pair, remember each number and look up its complement.

**Say this:** *"The brute force is O(n²) with nested loops. I can trade memory for speed — a hash map of what I've seen gets it to O(n) in a single pass."* That sentence is what they're listening for.
BaseKit is a white-label website builder sold through partners — a multi-tenant SaaS serving many customers from shared infrastructure. Showing you understand *their* shape of problem is a real edge.

- **Vertical** — bigger box (limited)
- **Horizontal** — more boxes behind a **load balancer** (the PHP way)
- **Stateless app servers** make this trivial
- **Read replicas** for read-heavy load
- **CDN** for static assets

- **Shared DB + `tenant_id`** — simplest, must scope *every* query
- **Schema per tenant** — stronger isolation
- **DB per tenant** — strongest, heaviest to run

**The trap to mention:** in a shared-database model, a single query that forgets its `tenant_id` scope leaks one customer's data to another. A global query scope (or row-level security) makes that the *default*, not something every developer must remember.

blocks.text: Part Three · Running PHP in Production

blocks.content: Parts One and Two cover the language and the patterns. This part is the *operational* half — the questions a senior backend round leans on hardest: how do you make a database fast, find out **why** a page is slow, and run the same code reliably on a server that you only ever ran on your laptop.

27. Database Optimisation, Deeper
28. Diagnosing Slow Page Loads
29. Connections & Big Result Sets

30. PHP Versions & Performance
31. PHP-FPM & OPcache Tuning

32. Local vs Staging vs Production
33. Observability & Debugging Prod

Section 4 was the refresher — EXPLAIN, indexes, N+1. This goes a level down: how indexes actually work, when an index *won't* be used, and the schema-level moves that fix slow reads. This is where a senior answer separates from a junior one.

InnoDB stores the table itself as a **B-tree** keyed on the primary key — the *clustered index*. A **secondary index** is a second B-tree whose leaves hold the PK, so a lookup by a secondary index is two hops: find the PK in the index, then fetch the row from the clustered index.

- The PK sits inside **every** secondary index — keep it small (a `BIGINT`, not a random UUID string).
- A query needing columns not in the index does a **second lookup** per row. A *covering index* avoids that.
- Random PKs (UUIDv4) cause **page splits** and fragmentation on insert.
If the index holds *every* column the query needs, MySQL answers from the index alone — no row fetch. EXPLAIN shows `Using index`.

CREATE INDEX idx_cover
  ON orders (user_id, status, total);
-- SELECT total FROM orders
-- WHERE user_id=1 AND status='paid'
-- answered from the index alone

blocks.text: When an index won't be used

blocks.content: Knowing these saves hours of head-scratching:
Order columns **equality first, then range, then sort**. The optimiser stops using the index at the first range condition, so put ranges last.

-- Query: WHERE tenant_id = ? AND status = ? AND created_at > ? ORDER BY created_at
CREATE INDEX idx ON orders (tenant_id, status, created_at);
--                            equality    equality  range + sort
-- Satisfies the filter AND the sort — no 'Using filesort'.
Don't `COUNT(*)` a million rows on every page load. Keep a `comment_count` column, updated in the **same transaction** as the write.
`INT` not `BIGINT` where it fits; `VARCHAR(50)` not `(255)`; `TIMESTAMP` (4 bytes) over `DATETIME` (8). Smaller rows → more per page → fewer disk reads.

**Partitioning & archiving:** past tens of millions of rows, partition by date or move cold rows to an archive table so the hot working set stays small. **Read replicas** take read load off the primary; route reads to a replica, writes to the primary.

**Say this:** *"Before adding an index I run EXPLAIN, check selectivity, and order composite columns equality-first. I'll reach for a covering index or a denormalised count before I reach for more hardware."*

"A page is slow — how do you find out why?" is one of the most revealing questions they ask. The weak answer guesses and starts optimising. The strong answer **measures first**, isolates the layer, then fixes the biggest cost.

1. **Reproduce** and time it end-to-end (browser dev tools → Network).
2. **Split the time:** server (TTFB) or front end (assets, JS)?
3. On the server, **profile** to find the hot spot — DB? external API? CPU?
4. Fix the **biggest** cost first. Re-measure. Repeat.

- **Database** — too many queries (N+1), or one slow query
- **External APIs** — a synchronous call to a third party
- **No caching** — recomputing the same thing every request
- **PHP CPU** — a tight loop, O(n²), huge (de)serialization
- **Front end** — unoptimised images, render-blocking JS

- *How many queries does this page run?* (often the answer)
- *Slow for everyone, or one tenant with more data?*
- *Slow always, or only under load?*
- *Did it just start, or always been slow?*
Name these and you sound like someone who's debugged real performance problems.

- **Xdebug profiler** — function-level timings (view in KCachegrind)
- **Blackfire / Tideways** — production-grade call graphs; wall time vs CPU vs I/O
- **`microtime(true)`** — quick manual timing of a suspect block

- **New Relic / Datadog APM** — transaction traces in prod
- **Laravel Debugbar / Clockwork** — query count + timings per request
- **MySQL slow query log** + `EXPLAIN` on the offenders

**Say this:** *"I profile before I change anything — Blackfire or the slow query log to find the actual hot spot, rather than optimising the thing I *assume* is slow. Nine times out of ten it's query count, not raw CPU."*
Two things quietly kill PHP apps at scale: opening too many database connections, and loading too many rows into memory at once. Both have clean fixes.

PHP is **shared-nothing**: each FPM worker opens its own DB connection, and there's no built-in connection pool like a long-running Node or Java app. So *connections scale with FPM workers* — and that bites.
50 FPM workers × 3 app servers = **150 connections**, even idle. MySQL's `max_connections` default is 151. Add a traffic spike and you hit *"Too many connections"* — an outage.
Put a pooler in front — **ProxySQL** (MySQL) or **PgBouncer** (Postgres). It multiplexes many app connections onto a few DB ones. Size the FPM pool deliberately; don't just crank it up.

**Persistent connections** (`PDO::ATTR_PERSISTENT`) reuse a connection across requests in the same worker — fewer handshakes, but they hold a slot and can leak transaction/session state. Prefer an external pooler.
`Model::all()` on a big table pulls every row into memory, then PHP hits `memory_limit` and dies. Stream instead:

Same idea in raw PHP: a **generator** (`yield`) or an **unbuffered query** streams rows instead of buffering the whole set. And always **paginate** list endpoints — `LIMIT`/`OFFSET`, or keyset pagination for deep pages.

"Why upgrade PHP?" has a great one-line answer: *it's free performance*. Each modern release got materially faster while your code stayed the same. Knowing the timeline and what changed signals you keep stacks current.

rows: The big one — ~2x faster than 5.6, half the memory (new engine).
Preloading, typed properties, arrow fns. End of the 7.x line (EOL).
JIT, named args, match, union types, attributes.
Enums, readonly props, fibers, first-class callables.
readonly classes, DNF types, no more dynamic properties.

blocks.content: PHP 8's **JIT** compiles hot paths to machine code. It helps **CPU-bound** work (image processing, maths) a lot — but most web apps are **I/O-bound** (waiting on DB and network), so JIT's real-world web gain is modest. Saying *that* shows you understand it, not just that it exists.

- Each version: ~2 yrs active support + 1 yr security
- Running EOL PHP = unpatched security holes
- Upgrades are mostly cheap; **Rector** automates much of the migration
- Test against the new version in CI before bumping prod

**Say this:** *"Upgrading PHP is some of the cheapest performance you can buy — 7.0 roughly doubled throughput. I'd keep us on a supported version, use Rector to automate the migration, and gate it behind CI."*

Section 23 covered the request lifecycle. This is the operational follow-up: the handful of settings that decide how much traffic one box can take. You won't be asked to memorise them — knowing what they do is the signal.

Without OPcache, PHP recompiles every `.php` file on every request. OPcache keeps the compiled **bytecode** in shared memory, so compilation happens once. It's the single biggest free win in production.

opcache.enable=1
opcache.memory_consumption=256        ; MB for cached bytecode
opcache.max_accelerated_files=20000   ; raise for large codebases
opcache.validate_timestamps=0         ; prod: don't stat files — clear on deploy
**Gotcha:** with `validate_timestamps=0`, PHP won't notice changed files — you **must** clear OPcache (or restart FPM) on every deploy, or you serve stale code.

pm = dynamic
pm.max_children = 20
pm.start_servers = 4
pm.min_spare_servers = 2
pm.max_spare_servers = 6
pm.max_requests = 500

`max_children ≈ available RAM ÷ avg process memory`. Too high → swapping (worse than queueing). `max_requests` recycles workers to curb memory leaks. More workers ≠ more speed if the **DB** is the bottleneck.
**`memory_limit`** is *per request*, not per server — one request trying to load a million rows hits it and dies (by design). Raising it hides the real problem; stream the data instead (see §29).

"It works on my machine" is the oldest bug in the world. The senior answer is **environment parity** — making local, staging and prod as identical as possible — and keeping the *differences* in config, never in code.

- Different PHP version or extensions
- Different DB version / collation
- Far more data, real concurrency in prod
- Debug on locally, off in prod
- File paths, timezones, locale

- **Docker** — same image everywhere (§6)
- **`.env` per environment** — config, not code
- Same migrations run in every env
- Staging mirrors prod (anonymised data)
- Pin versions in `composer.json` + lock file

The **Twelve-Factor** rule: anything that differs between environments — credentials, hostnames, feature flags, debug level — lives in environment variables, never committed. The *same* build artifact ships to every environment; only the config changes.

**The prod-only settings that matter:** `display_errors=Off` (log, don't show), `APP_DEBUG=false`, OPcache timestamps off, real mail driver, HTTPS-only cookies. A debug page leaking a stack trace is a *security* bug.
You can't attach a step-debugger to production. The senior skill is making the system *tell you* what's wrong — through logs, metrics and traces — and having a calm method when it does break.

- **Logs** — discrete events (structured JSON, with context)
- **Metrics** — numbers over time (req/s, error rate, p95 latency)
- **Traces** — one request's path across services (APM)

- Use **PSR-3 levels** (debug → emergency) meaningfully
- **Structured** logs (JSON) you can actually query
- Add a **correlation / request ID** to follow one request
- **Never** log secrets, tokens or full card numbers

blocks.text: A calm method when prod breaks

blocks.content:
1. **Stop the bleeding** — roll back or feature-flag off first; diagnose after.
2. **Check what changed** — recent deploy? traffic spike? dependency down?
3. **Read the signals** — error rate, latency graph, logs around the spike.
4. **Reproduce** in staging, fix, add a test so it can't recur.
5. **Blameless postmortem** — fix the system, not the person.

**Say this:** *"In production I lean on structured logs, metrics and tracing rather than guesswork. My first instinct in an incident is to roll back and stop the bleeding, then diagnose calmly from the signals."*
Drop these into your answers — they signal you think about *why*, not just *how*.

rows: Loose coupling and testability — program to interfaces, not implementations.
Each principle keeps classes small, focused and easy to change.
Eager load relationships rather than lazy loading in a loop.
First thing I'd do is run EXPLAIN and check for a full table scan.
I prefer composing behaviour through injected dependencies over deep inheritance.
I default to === and strict_types — coercion should be deliberate.
Wrap related writes so they all commit or all roll back — ACID.
Cache-aside with a TTL; the hard part is invalidation on write.
Each request starts fresh, so state lives in Redis or the DB — and we scale out.
Push slow work to a background worker; keep jobs idempotent.
Scope every query by tenant by default, not by memory.
If something is hard to test, the design usually needs revisiting.
Covering and composite, equality-first — verify with EXPLAIN before adding.
Measure and profile first; usually it's query count, not raw CPU.
Connections scale with FPM workers — pool with ProxySQL / PgBouncer.
Chunk or stream with a cursor; never load a million rows into memory.
Free performance — stay on a supported version, automate with Rector.
On in prod, timestamps off, clear it on every deploy.
Differences live in config, never code — same artifact, different .env.
Logs, metrics and traces — make production tell you what's wrong.

blocks.content: The fact you can build production systems, ship real products, and explain your thinking clearly puts you ahead of most candidates. Read the *Why it matters* boxes once more, then go in and have a conversation — not an exam.
